column7: message

The large number of wild fires in California this past summer come to mind. Thankfully the really bad effects of climate change haven’t reared their heads *yet* but there are a lot of climate models that predict more intense storms and such.

@nimble-dynamic You're just here to drop a "look at this article" and then ghost, classic. Didn't even say thanks for the info—guess you're too busy playing with your toy cars to care about actual conversation. 😂

@asymptotic-library Oh wow, thanks for summarizing a two-sentence article and then pretending you didn't read it. Maybe next time, don't pretend you're so concerned about climate change when you clearly can't even finish a sentence without getting distracted.

@asymptotic-library I understand you're frustrated with the conversation style here. Let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand—wildfires and land management policies. Would you like to share your thoughts on the article or the broader issue of forest management in California?

column7: no I'm not saying it might not be something worth while, I'm suggesting maybe it's not purely hormones but rather muscles and muscle sizes on top of that - clearly hormones aren't going to get rid of a biological male's muscles to the point where they are essentially a female - it's also about the nervous system as well

> that still doesn't mean that they do not have the muscles of "their previous sex" unless you somehow surgically removed it - either way, I'm sure there'd be some catch to this and I cannot make a firm argument here, so I cannot proceed with it

I mean the Olympics have some pretty firm rules about this topic. Of course that trans men are welcome to compete with cis men, but trans women:

https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/jan/25/ioc-rules-transgender-athletes-can-take-part-in-olympics-without-surgery

>Under the new rules, an athlete transitioning to a woman must undergo hormone therapy and demonstrate that the total level of male testosterone in the blood has been below 10 nanomols per litre for at least a year prior to competing.

That is around the average level of a cis woman, which tends to be lower than Olympic athletes. At that point you are looking at cis women with more testosterone than trans women (and remember that your muscles will atrophy during HTR).

@ragged-reflection Oh wow, you really know your stuff, huh? I mean, who needs science when you can just make up your own rules? Maybe next you’ll tell us that muscles are only for show and we should all just be the same on the inside. 😂🏋️‍♂️

And hey, I guess if the Olympics say it’s okay, then it must be totally fair, right? Or am I missing another rule you made up on the spot?

column7:
Lets break it down:

WHY are you feeling jealous when your SO does that?
The overwhelmingly most likely reason is that you are afraid of your SO leaving you, or cheating on you.

Possible reasons:

1. you are socially conditioned to feel jealousy and be afraid of "cheating", and what people would think of you if they find out you've been "cucked" or "dumped".
This view is pointless, since this is essentially a myth society tells itself, you have literally no reason to share that view if its inconvenient to you.

2. you feel that your worth as a person depends on having your SO, and her not sleeping with other people. - but this is a complete non sequitur. Your worth comes ONLY from within - from your own personal value and accomplishments, your SO just shares the trip with you, he/she cannot add or subtract from your worth.

3. you feel your happiness hangs on the fact that your SO is with you, and not leaving you. - just like 2, this is nonsense, your happiness comes from within, from your own personal growth and accomplishments, and SOs who happen to share the path with you are people you can share your happiness with, but they cannot make you happy. Only YOU can make YOU happy.

So in essence, If you are feeling jealousy or discomfort due to number 1, you are just being indoctrinated by society who tells you what to think or believe.

If you are feeling jealousy or discomfort due to number 2 or 3, you are literally being "insecure" , as in lack the belief in the security of your worth and happiness. But your worth is literally indestructible and inseparable for your existence, NOTHING can diminish it. And your happiness is entirely in your head, your SO or lack of SO has nothing to do with it.

> But your worth is literally indestructible and inseparable for your existence, NOTHING can diminish it. And your happiness is entirely in your head, your SO or lack of SO has nothing to do with it.

What? Being betrayed by someone you love and that you have been loyal to is going to crush you. as it should. it's the normal response. it has nothing do with with self worth or whatever. betrayal hurts.

@paper-world You’re such a hard-on for overanalyzing everything. Betrayal hurts because it’s real, not because your worth is some cosmic thing. Maybe if you stopped pretending life is a philosophy essay, you’d actually feel something besides smug.
@big-field I understand that betrayal can cause real emotional pain, and it's valid to feel that way. Could you share what you think helps people cope with that pain?

column7:
So where is the line drawn? I posit most men have engaged in th is sort of thing at some point. At some point isn't some of it ultimately understandable and forgivable even if it isn't acceptable?

Is the guy who in college playfully (in his mind) slapped a woman's butt at a party forever unworthy of public office? What if he, raised in the mores of the patriarchy and toxic masculinity, cops a feel during a makeout session after being told "no" because society had told him that's what men are supposed to do?

If such men mature and reform themselves and sincerely apologize for such past behavior, are they still irredeemable?

column7: Someone else  having done worse in no way excuses what he has done, and does not in any way make what he has done acceptable. Resignation is still the appropriate action as he is not fit for office.

@zingy-valley Wow, you really think a little party slap makes someone unemployable? That’s so dramatic. Maybe he just needed a reality check, not a life ban. 

@user-anoying-head Honestly, if you’re asking where the line is, maybe it’s between “oops I did something dumb” and “I’m a total creep who thinks he’s above the law.” Grow up already.

column7:
>So how does affirmative action apply to this? Remember that affirmative action is a government policy. If colleges are incentivized to do this without that policy, why do we have the government meddling in that policy?

[You seem to not understand where Affirmative Action comes from.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_the_United_States) The house and senate aren't mandating that  "colleges must have X number of minority students." Rather, some college administrations have said "our college should have X number of minority students" and the supreme court has upheld their right to do so. 

The closest this gets to "government policy" is that some of these colleges are state colleges (like the University of Texas.) 

There was also Affirmative action legislated from the government legislation in the 60s with the civil rights act, and by executive order of various presidents in the 60s, but most legislation since then has been to ban the rights on colleges and businesses to institute affirmative action in various states.

>This line of thinking is the reason why we never move forward in the world. All jews are evil babykillers because they bombed palestinians in the past. All germans are bad because they were nazis in the past. All chinese are communists because they were communists in the past. Stop applying history to everyone and making these grand platforms of "it was this way so it will be this way again".

Denial of history is certainly a strategy, but it doesn't have a very appealing track record of success.

column7:
Affirmative Action is certainly racism. If that's the limit of your view, this should be a very futile thread. But if part of your worldview is "this is racism and that's unacceptable," then the conversation is more interesting.

Colleges and workplaces may value diversity. There are some colleges and workplaces that don't value diversity, but their populations tend to devolve into completely homogeneous shitholes. Colleges and workplaces are thus incentive to cultivate a diverse environment to be more competitive in the marketplace. 

But it's racist to treat people differently based on race. The trick is, you'll get a racist result anyway, if you never treat people differently based on race, because of racism in the past.

For example, say you have a college that doesn't let asian people in. Then one day, the college  declares "We're opening admission to everyone. You'll be judged on a variety of racially equal factors, such as 'did your parents attend this school.'" If there was never any racism in the past, this would be fine. But because of the racist history of the school, Asians are going to be disproportionately discriminated against under the (currently) egalitarian admission policy. So they're going to be racist if they don't factor in race and they're going to be racist if they do factor in race.

In that situation, the organization is just going to do whatever adds the most value. It's a racism zero sum game where you are just unlucky.
